Macedonius having possessed himself of the See of Constantinople inflicts much Injury on those who differ from him.

ccel.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

title: Macedonius having possessed himself of the See of Constantinople inflicts much Injury on those who differ from him.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: At that time Paul having been removed in the manner described, Macedonius became ruler of the churches in Constantinople; who, acquiring very great ascendancy over the emperor, stirred up a war among Christians, of a no less grievous kind than that which the usurpers themselves were waging.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For having prevailed on his sovereign to co-operate with him in devastating the churches, he procured that whatever pernicious measures he determined to pursue should be ratified by law.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: And on this account throughout the several cities an edict was proclaimed, and a military force appointed to carry the imperial decrees into effect.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Accordingly those who acknowledged the doctrine of consubstantiality were expelled not only from the churches, but also from the cities.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Now at first they were satisfied with expulsion; but as the evil grew they resorted to the worse extremity of inducing compulsory communion with them, caring but little for such a desecration of the churches.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Their violence indeed was scarcely less than that of those who had formerly obliged the Christians to worship idols; for they applied all kinds of scourgings, a variety of tortures, and confiscation of property.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Many were punished with exile; some died under the torture; and others were put to death while they were being led into exile.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: These atrocities were exercised throughout all the eastern cities, but especially at Constantinople; the internal strife which was but slight before was thus savagely increased by Macedonius, as soon as he obtained the bishopric.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The cities of Greece, however, and Illyricum, with those of the western parts, still enjoyed tranquillity; inasmuch as they preserved harmony among themselves, and continued to adhere to the rule of faith promulgated by the council of Nicæa.
